title: Chiles-Whitted UAP sighting report (1948)
content: On July 24 1948, Eastern Air Lines pilots Clarence Chiles and John Whitted reported encountering a rocket-shaped object near Montgomery Alabama. The official Air Force report describes the object as cigar-shaped approximately 100 feet long with two rows of windows emitting a bright glow and a trail of orange-red flame from the rear. The case was classified as unidentified in official Air Force records. No determination of origin or cause was ever made.

title: Washington D.C. radar UAP incidents (1952)
content: In July 1952, multiple unidentified targets were tracked on radar over Washington D.C. airspace on two separate weekends. Air Traffic Control at Washington National Airport and Andrews Air Force Base both confirmed the radar returns. Intercepting F-94 jets found nothing visually. The official Air Force explanation cited temperature inversions causing radar anomalies, though investigators noted the explanation did not account for all observed characteristics. The case remains in official records as investigated but not conclusively explained.

title: Malmstrom Air Force Base UAP incident report (1967)
content: In March 1967, security personnel at Malmstrom AFB in Montana reported a glowing red object hovering above the facility. Shortly after the sighting, ten Minuteman nuclear missiles in the Echo Flight went into a No-Go status simultaneously. The official Air Force investigation found no confirmed causal link between the reported UAP and the missile failures. The incident is documented in declassified Air Force records as unexplained.

title: Apollo 11 post-flight debriefing (1969)
content: In a 1969 post-flight debriefing, astronaut Buzz Aldrin described seeing brief flashes of light inside the cabin spaced a couple of minutes apart while trying to sleep, and separately described a bright light source the crew tentatively attributed to a possible laser. The document records this as an observation, with no determination of cause.

title: Apollo 12 mission record (1969)
content: During the 1969 Apollo 12 mission, astronaut Alan Bean reported flashes of light that appeared to sail off into space. A separate photo from the Apollo 12 landing site shows a highlighted area of interest slightly above the horizon containing an unidentified visual phenomenon. No cause is identified in the record.

title: Borman bogey sighting transcript (1969)
content: A mission transcript records astronaut Frank Borman reporting a bogey and a debris field described as consisting of hundreds of small particles. A handwritten annotation on the document is labeled UFO Sighting by Borman. The case remains formally unresolved.

title: USS Nimitz Tic Tac incident official report (2004)
content: In November 2004, Navy pilots from the USS Nimitz Carrier Strike Group encountered an unidentified aerial vehicle approximately 40 feet long described as resembling a Tic Tac. The object demonstrated no visible propulsion, no wings, and no exhaust. It was tracked on the carrier SPY-1 radar for approximately two weeks prior to the encounter. The FLIR1 video was later officially released by the Department of Defense. The Navy formally classified the object as an unidentified aerial phenomenon. No conventional explanation has been established.

title: Gimbal and Go Fast UAP FLIR videos (2014-2015)
content: Two infrared videos from 2014 and 2015 designated Gimbal and Go Fast were officially released by the Department of Defense in 2020. The Gimbal video shows a rotating object with no visible propulsion moving against the wind. The Go Fast video shows an object moving at high speed at low altitude over the ocean. Both were recorded by Navy aircraft FLIR systems. The Department of Defense confirmed the objects in both videos remain unidentified. No conventional explanation has been publicly established for either.

title: Former President Obama public statement on UAP (2021)
content: In a 2021 television appearance, former President Barack Obama stated there are footage and records of objects in the skies that we do not know exactly what they are and we cannot explain how they moved or their trajectory. Obama stated he had asked about UAP when he was in office and was told there was a small group studying the issue. He explicitly did not claim the objects were extraterrestrial and noted there were things he could not share publicly.

title: Unresolved UAP report, Kuwait theater (2022)
content: A 2022 unidentified aerial phenomenon report from the Kuwait theater includes an image showing an elongated area of contrast that increases in intensity along its length. The Department of Defense classifies the case as unresolved due to insufficient evidence to determine the object's nature.

title: AARO Annual Report on UAP (2023)
content: The All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office AARO 2023 annual report to Congress stated that as of December 2023 AARO had received over 800 UAP reports. Of these approximately 2 to 5 percent were characterized as demonstrating genuinely anomalous flight characteristics that could not be attributed to known human technology or natural phenomena. The report explicitly stated that AARO had found no evidence of extraterrestrial origin for any UAP. The majority of cases were attributed to sensor artifacts, weather, or conventional aircraft.

title: David Grusch congressional testimony (2023)
content: In July 2023 testimony before a House subcommittee, former intelligence official David Grusch stated his belief that the U.S. government possesses non-human origin craft and biological material. Grusch acknowledged he had not personally seen such materials but had received information from other officials. The Department of Defense formally denied Grusch's claims. No physical evidence was presented during the testimony. The official government position remains that no confirmed evidence of non-human intelligence has been established.

title: U.S. Northern Command infrared sensor report (2024)
content: U.S. Northern Command submitted a 21-second video captured by an infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform in 2024, documenting an unidentified anomalous phenomenon. The report does not draw a conclusion about the object's origin or nature.

title: Classified Senate UAP briefing summary (2025)
content: A 2025 classified briefing to Senate Intelligence Committee members subsequently partially declassified noted that intelligence community assessment of UAP remained inconclusive across all investigated cases. The document stated that no UAP had been confirmed as originating from a foreign adversary nation. It further noted that certain cases involving transmedium travel remain unexplained. The briefing explicitly stated no confirmation of extraterrestrial origin exists in the intelligence community holdings.

title: Official PURSUE program summary statement (2026)
content: The Department of War and Department of Defense initial PURSUE tranche contains 162 declassified files spanning 1948 to 2026 sourced from the Department of Defense, FBI, NASA, and State Department. Officials describe the release as a collection of unresolved cases for which the government could not reach a definitive determination based on available evidence, and have explicitly stated the files do not confirm the existence of extraterrestrial life. The public is invited to draw its own conclusions.
